title: Adjusted trajectory of medication exposure taking into account the periodicity of dispensations and the number of dispensed packs and comparative analysis on EFEMERIS database
summary: We presented an adjustment method for the calculation of medication exposure trajectories based on the number of dispensed packs and the type of dispensations (occasional or regular). A comparative study based on the EFEMERIS data was carried out using three different scenarios of trajectory calculation depending on whether or not the number of packs and the periodicity of medication dispensations were taken into account. The impact of the scenario was highlighted using global indicators on the number of Define-Daily Dose (DDD) on all women exposed; the study of changes in individual trajectories from one scenario to another was carried out; we also compared the results of a clustering into four groups. If 65% of the trajectories remained unchanged, we could observe on the rest significant changes in number of DDD and/or on individual exposure profile. We observed 4% of trajectories that were attributed to a different cluster, and the clustering was of better quality with the adjustment method. Depending on the study context, an impact on cluster distribution could be observed for some maternal characteristics and neonatal outcomes. This was the case for a higher occurrence of neonatal pathology for neonates from mothers belonging to the cluster with high doses of psychotropics, thus reinforcing the conclusions of previous studies of a link between high exposure to psychotropic medications and presence of pathology for the newborn.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an adjustment method for calculating medication exposure trajectories that accounts for number of dispensed packs and dispensation type (occasional vs. regular). Using EFEMERIS data, they compared three scenarios and found that while 65% of trajectories remained unchanged, the adjustment method improved clustering quality and affected associations with neonatal outcomes, particularly reinforcing links between high psychotropic exposure and neonatal pathology.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy of medication exposure trajectory calculations by accounting for both the number of dispensed packs and the periodicity of dispensations (occasional vs. regular), as standard methods may misclassify or obscure important exposure patterns.
AI.method: Comparative study using EFEMERIS data with three scenarios of trajectory calculation (varying whether pack counts and dispensation periodicity were considered). Analysis included global DDD indicators, individual trajectory changes, and clustering into four groups.
AI.result: 65% of trajectories remained unchanged; 4% of trajectories changed cluster assignment; clustering quality improved with the adjustment method; significant changes in DDD and individual exposure profiles were observed in the remaining trajectories; association between high psychotropic exposure and neonatal pathology was reinforced.
AI.conclusion: The adjustment method improves trajectory calculation quality and can affect study conclusions regarding associations between medication exposure and health outcomes, particularly reinforcing the link between high-dose psychotropic exposure during pregnancy and neonatal pathology.

title: When is Enough Enough? A Proposed Termination Point for the Number of Replicates in Computational Simulations
summary: Computational simulation provides a powerful toolkit for in silico experimentation. However, while the field has developed best practices for the design and implementation of such models, there remains ambiguity in discussions about how to understand and/or interpret their results due to their inherent ability to overwhelm traditional frequentist statistics by simply increasing the number of trials simulated. This fails the discipline in two ways: first, it leaves the community unsure of what constitutes a best practice for uniform understanding, and second, it potentially overburdens computational studies that burn clock cycles solely to ensure "enough runs to satisfy peers" without any theoretical underpinning for a definition of "enough". We propose a simple and straightforward standard for when to stop simulating additional trials, the Ω test, designed to be analogous to the function of traditional frequentist P-tests. Community adoption of a reasonable and uniform standard will permit more efficient computational experimentation and clearly communication/interpretation of the findings discovered in this way.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Ω test, a standardized stopping criterion for computational simulations that is analogous to frequentist P-tests, to address the problem of simulations being run excessively just to satisfy peer expectations without theoretical justification.
AI.motivation: Computational simulations can overwhelm traditional frequentist statistics by simply increasing trial numbers, leading to ambiguity in interpreting results, lack of best practices, and wasteful over-simulation just to satisfy peers without theoretical grounding for what constitutes "enough" runs.
AI.method: The authors propose the Ω test, a straightforward standard analogous to traditional frequentist P-tests, to determine when to stop simulating additional trials in computational experiments.
AI.result: The Ω test provides a theoretically grounded stopping criterion for simulations, enabling more efficient computational experimentation while maintaining statistical rigor.
AI.conclusion: Adopting a uniform standard like the Ω test will allow more efficient computational experimentation and clearer communication/interpretation of simulation findings.

title: Multifractal Signatures of Ageing and Dementia Development: A Multifractal Space-Filling Curve Analysis
summary: Multifractality is an effective formalism for quantifying the nonlinear, scale-free properties of complex data. In this study, we propose a novel and efficient methodology, termed Multifractal Space-filling Curve Analysis (MFSCA), for quantifying the correlation structure of multidimensional data. Within this framework, the original multidimensional data - while preserving both local and long-range organisational properties - are projected onto a one-dimensional representation using a fractal space-filling curve. The resulting one-dimensional signal is then analysed using multifractal algorithms. We demonstrate the utility of the method using both artificially generated multifractal structures and real data. In particular, we apply MFSCA to analyse magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data from Alzheimer patients at different stages of dementia. Based on the results, we estimate the multifractal profiles of the brain for healthy subjects of different ages as well as for dementia patients. The analysis reveals that the spatial organization of brain structures, as measured by the degree of multifractality, progressively weakens with age and the development of dementia. A transition from multifractality to monofractality is observed both in control groups, when comparing the Young Control and Elderly Control groups, and among dementia subjects of similar age but at different stages of the disease, namely early dementia and mild cognitive impairment. Thus, from the perspective of multiscaling properties, the heterogeneous characteristics of spatial brain organization deteriorate under worsening conditions, leading to a homogeneous and weakly correlated structure. These findings not only effectively capture key aspects of brain organisation, but also demonstrate that the multifractality of MRI data can serve as a marker of structural brain changes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MFSCA, a method that projects multidimensional data onto 1D using fractal space-filling curves and applies multifractal analysis. Applied to MRI data from Alzheimer's patients, it shows that brain spatial organization shifts from multifractal to monofractal with aging and dementia progression, suggesting multifractality as a potential biomarker.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient methodology for quantifying correlation structures of multidimensional data using multifractal formalism, and to demonstrate its utility in detecting structural brain changes associated with aging and dementia.
AI.method: MFSCA projects multidimensional data onto a 1D representation using fractal space-filling curves (preserving local and long-range organization), then applies multifractal algorithms to the resulting 1D signal.
AI.result: Spatial organization of brain structures progressively weakens with age and dementia. A transition from multifractality to monofractality is observed between Young vs Elderly controls, and between early dementia vs mild cognitive impairment patients.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous brain organization deteriorates under worsening conditions toward homogeneous, weakly correlated structures. Multifractality of MRI data can serve as a marker of structural brain changes.

title: Hyperbolic Neural Population Geometry Benefits Computation
summary: Neural population geometry shapes downstream computation. Recent empirical findings in neurobiology suggest that a hyperbolic structure underlies population activity in the hippocampus. Here we provide a theoretical framework for this phenomenon. First, we propose a plausible construction of hippocampal tuning curves that statistically induces hyperbolic geometry. Next, we establish a connection between neural decoding and associative memory by demonstrating that the Modern Hopfield Network update rule computes the minimum mean-squared-error (MMSE) estimator. Finally, we introduce a novel associative memory model defined in hyperbolic space that yields significantly larger capacity than leading models. Our results suggest that animals encode spatial information as a latent hyperbolic cognitive map, improving both memory capacity and decoding accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that hippocampal neural activity follows hyperbolic geometry, showing how this structure improves memory capacity and decoding accuracy through a theoretical framework connecting tuning curves, decoding, and associative memory.
AI.motivation: To provide a theoretical explanation for recent neurobiological findings suggesting hyperbolic geometry in hippocampal population activity.
AI.method: The authors (1) construct hippocampal tuning curves that induce hyperbolic geometry, (2) connect neural decoding to associative memory by showing Modern Hopfield Networks compute MMSE estimators, and (3) introduce a hyperbolic-space associative memory model.
AI.result: The hyperbolic associative memory model achieves significantly larger memory capacity than leading existing models.
AI.conclusion: Animals encode spatial information as latent hyperbolic cognitive maps, which improves both memory capacity and decoding accuracy in the hippocampus.

title: Modeling pest dynamics in trap cropping to improve yield: the effects of attraction, retention, and land allocation
summary: Trap crops reduce damage to a cash (main) crop by attracting pests away from it. Yet this protection is weakened when pests disperse back into the cash crop. In this paper, we focus on the importance of preventing this backflow, showing that effective trap cropping depends jointly on how strongly pests are attracted to trap plants and how rarely they leave them. Together with the proportion of the field devoted to trap plants, these processes determine both the efficacy and feasibility of trap cropping at commercial scales. We formalise this relationship using a simple yield-maximisation framework, in which growers weigh pest suppression benefits against the land sacrificed to trap plants. The model shows that when dispersal from trap plants equals that from the cash crop, optimal trap coverage can exceed 20 to 30 percent of the landscape, levels rarely acceptable to growers. However, reducing pest dispersal off trap plants to just one-quarter of cash crop dispersal lowers the optimal required trap area to approximately 5 percent, transforming trap cropping from impractical to feasible. Understanding these relationships can guide trap-cropping design, from plant choice to targeted interventions that reduce pest movement, to minimise damage, maximise yield, and make trap cropping a reliable component of sustainable pest management.

AI.tldr: Trap cropping effectiveness depends on pest attraction to trap plants and how rarely pests leave them. A yield-maximization model shows that reducing pest dispersal from trap plants to 25% of cash crop levels lowers optimal trap area from >20-30% to ~5%, making trap cropping commercially feasible.
AI.motivation: To quantify why trap cropping often fails commercially (pests disperse back to cash crops) and determine the key factors that make it practically feasible at scale.
AI.method: A simple yield-maximisation framework that models the trade-off between pest suppression benefits and land sacrificed to trap plants, incorporating pest attraction strength and dispersal rates.
AI.result: When pest dispersal from trap plants equals cash crop dispersal, optimal trap coverage >20-30% (impractical). Reducing dispersal to 25% of cash crop level reduces optimal trap area to ~5% (feasible).
AI.conclusion: Trap cropping viability hinges on minimizing pest backflow; understanding dispersal dynamics can guide plant choice and interventions to make trap cropping a reliable sustainable pest management strategy.

title: Compositional proofreading through critical self-tuning
summary: High-dimensional multicomponent systems, including immune and epigenetic repertoires, must selectively retain rare, beneficial components while purging a massive influx of suboptimal variants. We demonstrate that critical tuning of component control parameters through competition naturally implements proofreading in these systems. Competition for shared inputs pins the system to the marginal stability threshold of the most persistent species. This grants dominant species extended lifetimes, concentrating the population into dominant components while forcing less-stable variants into rapid drift-driven turnover. When aggregate drive exceeds a characteristic scale, this pinning fails, producing a non-selective state where component lifetimes scale as a universal power law with aggregate drive. Applying this framework to biological memory, we identify the hallmarks of this effect in plasma cell accumulation dynamics and propose that de-pinning transitions may represent failure points across biological domains, including cancer, immunodeficiencies, and the aberrant activation of harmful genomic elements during ageing.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that biological systems like immune and epigenetic repertoires achieve selective retention of rare, beneficial components through a competition-driven critical tuning mechanism akin to proofreading. Competition pins the system at a marginal stability threshold, extending dominant species' lifetimes and causing less-stable variants to turn over rapidly. When aggregate drive exceeds a critical scale, selectivity fails and component lifetimes follow a universal power law. The framework explains plasma cell accumulation dynamics and suggests that de-pinning transitions may underlie failures in cancer, immunodeficiencies, and genomic element activation during aging.
AI.motivation: To understand how high-dimensional multicomponent biological systems (e.g., immune and epigenetic repertoires) selectively retain rare beneficial components while eliminating a large influx of suboptimal variants.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate that critical tuning of component control parameters through competition naturally implements proofreading. Competition for shared resources pins the system to the marginal stability threshold of the most persistent species, creating a selective retention mechanism.
AI.result: Dominant species gain extended lifetimes, concentrating the population into dominant components while unstable variants undergo rapid drift-driven turnover. When aggregate drive exceeds a characteristic scale, selectivity fails and component lifetimes follow a universal power law.
AI.conclusion: This framework explains plasma cell accumulation dynamics and proposes that de-pinning transitions may represent failure points in cancer, immunodeficiencies, and aberrant activation of harmful genomic elements during aging.

title: Chaos and stability in the marine trophic network: the importance of interactions over complexity
summary: Understanding the dynamics of real world complex networks is crucial for assessing their predictability, resilience, and improving ecosystem management, especially in the context of climate change. The relationship between stability and complexity in ecological networks is still debated in the literature. In this modeling study, we investigate whether a complex marine trophic network, characterized by multiple trophic interactions and environmental constraints, exhibits predominantly stable, periodic or chaotic dynamics. We incorporate the microbial loop into a trophic network model, which includes one to three primary producers, one or two consumers, and up to three trophic levels of predators. The microbial loop is a key process in which bacteria recycle detritus from higher trophic levels into nutrients available for the growth of primary producers, ensuring mass conservation within the system. We perform numerical simulations to investigate the dynamic behavior of the network, exploring several configurations by turning off predator prey links between species and varying the high dimensional parameter space. Our results show that (i) longer trophic chains and (ii) a higher number of consumers increase system chaoticity, whereas (iii) omnivorous interactions promote stability. Notably, many of the configurations exhibit high percentages of chaotic behavior. Feedback loop analysis suggests that the balance between negative and positive interactions plays a key role in the convergence of the system toward a steady state. This study shows that interactions and feedback, rather than complexity, are key drivers of stability, pointing to the absence of a clear stability complexity relationship and instead highlighting a stability interaction dependence. Chaotic dynamics may also play an important role, with potential implications for predictability and ecosystem management.

AI.tldr: This modeling study investigates stability-complexity relationships in marine trophic networks incorporating the microbial loop, finding that longer trophic chains and more consumers increase chaoticity while omnivorous interactions promote stability, with interactions and feedback being key drivers rather than complexity itself.
AI.motivation: To resolve the ongoing debate about the relationship between stability and complexity in ecological networks, and understand what drives dynamics (stable, periodic, or chaotic) in real-world complex marine trophic systems, especially under climate change contexts.
AI.method: A modeling study incorporating the microbial loop into a trophic network with 1-3 primary producers, 1-2 consumers, and up to 3 predator trophic levels. Numerical simulations were performed with various configurations by removing predator-prey links and varying high-dimensional parameter space to analyze dynamic behavior.
AI.result: (i) Longer trophic chains and (ii) higher number of consumers increase chaoticity; (iii) omnivorous interactions promote stability. Many configurations showed high percentages of chaotic behavior. Balance between negative and positive feedback loops was key for steady-state convergence.
AI.conclusion: Interactions and feedback, rather than complexity per se, are the key drivers of stability in ecological networks. There is no clear stability-complexity relationship; instead, there is a stability-interaction dependence. Chaotic dynamics have important implications for predictability and ecosystem management.

title: Spatial Model Selection and Uncertainty Quantification: Comparing Continuous and Discrete Wound Healing Models
summary: All data-driven modeling tasks (e.g., parameter estimation, uncertainty quantification, and data forecasting) require the selection of a mathematical model. An overlooked aspect of model selection is modality; for example, there are no guidelines on when to use a partial differential equation (PDE) model or an agent-based model (ABM) for spatial processes. To address this, we created a model selection pipeline that uses approximate Bayesian computations to perform parameter estimation, uncertainty quantification, and model selection (using both information criteria and out-of-sample forecasting). Applying the pipeline to artificial datasets (generated from ABMs) reveals that while both modalities yield comparable parameter estimation performance, the ABM estimates exhibit higher uncertainty, and the PDE models compute more than 1,000$\times$ faster. Surprisingly, the mean-field PDE is often selected over the true generative ABM model using both information criteria and data forecasting. Applying the pipeline to public wound healing data indicates that a PDE model with cell pulling and a time delay is the most appropriate model for this data, however, this model has high levels of parametric uncertainty. This methodology establishes a preliminary framework for selecting the appropriate modeling modality for spatial biological data.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a model selection pipeline using approximate Bayesian computations to compare PDE and agent-based models for spatial processes, finding that while both perform similarly in parameter estimation, PDEs are much faster and often preferred over ABMs even when ABMs generate the data.
AI.motivation: There are no guidelines on when to use a PDE model versus an ABM for spatial processes, despite modality being an overlooked aspect of model selection.
AI.method: A model selection pipeline using approximate Bayesian computations for parameter estimation, uncertainty quantification, and model selection (via information criteria and out-of-sample forecasting).
AI.result: Both modalities yield comparable parameter estimation, but ABM estimates have higher uncertainty while PDE models compute 1,000× faster. Mean-field PDE is often selected over the true generative ABM. Application to wound healing data identifies a PDE model with cell pulling and time delay as most appropriate.
AI.conclusion: This establishes a preliminary framework for selecting the appropriate modeling modality for spatial biological data.

title: From the microscope to High Performance Computing centers, a national effort toward automated data workflows for microscopy facility users in France
summary: Modern biological microscopy routinely generates large and complex image datasets, including multidimensional, multimodal, and time-resolved acquisitions. While imaging technologies have rapidly evolved, data management infrastructures within microscopy facilities often remain fragmented, relying on heterogeneous local solutions that are difficult to maintain, scale, and integrate with High-Performance Computing (HPC) centers and public data repositories. To address these issues, France BioImaging (FBI), the French national infrastructure for biological imaging, has developed FBI.DATA and the associated BioImage Cloud platform. This initiative aims to provide a coordinated national infrastructure connecting microscopy facilities, centralized storage resources, HPC environments, and public bioimaging archives through interoperable and scalable workflows.The proposed architecture combines open-source technologies including OMERO for image management, iRODS for distributed data orchestration, Authentik for federated authentication, and emerging standards such as OME-Zarr and REMBI metadata recommendations. The infrastructure is designed to support the complete imaging data lifecycle, from acquisition and transfer to visualization, analysis, sharing, and long-term archiving. Beyond the technical implementation, this work presents the organizational and governance strategies required to deploy a shared national infrastructure across distributed imaging facilities. We discuss the challenges associated with interoperability, metadata standardization, sustainability, and user adoption, as well as the perspectives opened by tighter integration between imaging data and large-scale computing resources for future AI-driven bioimage analysis workflows.

AI.tldr: France BioImaging developed FBI.DATA and BioImage Cloud, a national infrastructure connecting microscopy facilities to HPC and public archives using OMERO, iRODS, OME-Zarr, and REMBI standards, supporting the full imaging data lifecycle.
AI.motivation: Modern microscopy generates complex datasets but data management infrastructures remain fragmented, hard to scale, and poorly integrated with HPC centers and public repositories.
AI.method: The architecture combines open-source tools: OMERO for image management, iRODS for data orchestration, Authentik for authentication, OME-Zarr format, and REMBI metadata standards to support the full data lifecycle.
AI.result: A coordinated national infrastructure connecting distributed microscopy facilities with centralized storage, HPC, and public archives through interoperable workflows.
AI.conclusion: The work discusses challenges of interoperability, metadata standardization, sustainability, and user adoption, while highlighting future potential for AI-driven bioimage analysis through tighter HPC integration.

title: Sleep EEG Signal Criticality as a Non-Invasive Predictor of Cognitive Decline in Dementia
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, accepted for publication in the Proc. 48th Annu. Int. Conf. IEEE EMBS (EMBC 2026), Toronto, Canada, July 20-24, 2026
summary: Early detection of neurodegeneration remains a critical clinical challenge. This study investigates whether sleep EEG signal criticality, quantified via Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MFDFA), serves as a non-invasive biomarker for future cognitive decline. We analyzed longitudinal data from the National Sleep Research Resource (NSRR) Study of Osteoporotic Fractures (SOF) cohort, comparing baseline sleep EEG dynamics between women who remained cognitively normal and those who later progressed to dementia-related impairment ($3MS < 78$).Our results reveal significant group-level differences in Hurst exponent $H(q)$ distributions, particularly during non-REM stages N2 and N3. Cognitively healthy individuals exhibited signal dynamics significantly closer to an optimally critical state across all electrode locations ($p \leqslant 0.001$), supporting the Brain Criticality Hypothesis. Supervised UMAP projections confirmed clear spatial separation between groups throughout the overnight sleep architecture.The dementia group demonstrated a shift in DFA exponents toward $1.0$, suggesting that a reconfiguration of scale-free neural dynamics during sleep precedes clinical symptoms. These findings highlight the potential for MFDFA-derived measures to be integrated into automated, sleep-based screening tools, enabling earlier preventative interventions during the prodromal window of dementia.

AI.tldr: This study shows that sleep EEG criticality measured via MFDFA can distinguish women who later develop dementia from cognitively healthy controls, suggesting a non-invasive early biomarker for neurodegeneration.
AI.motivation: Early detection of neurodegeneration remains a critical clinical challenge; non-invasive biomarkers are needed to identify individuals at risk before symptoms manifest.
AI.method: Used Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MFDFA) on sleep EEG data from the NSRR SOF cohort, comparing baseline sleep dynamics between cognitively normal women and those who later progressed to dementia (3MS < 78). Applied supervised UMAP projections for visualization.
AI.result: Significant group-level differences in Hurst exponent H(q) distributions during N2 and N3 sleep; cognitively healthy individuals showed dynamics closer to optimal criticality (p ≤ 0.001). Dementia group showed DFA exponents shifting toward 1.0. UMAP projections confirmed clear spatial separation between groups.
AI.conclusion: MFDFA-derived measures can potentially be integrated into automated sleep-based screening tools for earlier preventative interventions during the prodromal window of dementia.

title: Bilinear gating of motor primitives: a principle linking dendritic computation to rapid goal-directed adaptation
summary: Movement requires the motor cortex to specify both \emph{what} action to produce and \emph{which goal} it serves, yet how individual neurons separate these factors is not understood. Here we show that in macaque motor cortex the \emph{burst fraction} of a neuron, the proportion of its spikes emitted in high-frequency bursts, encodes reach direction far more selectively than its overall firing rate. This dissociation is highly consistent: it holds in every one of 12 recording sessions spanning three animals and two laboratories (all $p<10^{-12}$) and survives controls that remove any contribution of firing rate, showing that goal information is concentrated specifically in bursts. We then show that this coding signature is the predicted consequence of dendritic coincidence detection in layer-5 pyramidal neurons: when a goal-related apical input coincides with a state-related basal drive the neuron bursts, so burst probability computes the product of goal and state, a bilinear gate $G(g)\,Y(s)$. A minimal two-compartment spiking model reproduces the effect, and the same multiplicative gate, embedded in a reinforcement-learning agent, supports zero-shot generalisation to new goals and rapid online adaptation, providing a computational rationale for segregating goal information into bursts. These results identify burst fraction as a goal-selective code in motor cortex, tie it to a concrete cellular mechanism, and show that the mechanism confers a learning advantage.

AI.tldr: Burst fraction in motor cortex neurons encodes reach goals more selectively than firing rate, explained by dendritic coincidence detection, and supports zero-shot generalization in RL agents.
AI.motivation: The motor cortex must encode both *what* action to perform and *which goal* it serves, but how individual neurons separate these factors is unknown.
AI.method: Analyzed burst fraction vs. firing rate in macaque motor cortex across 12 sessions (3 animals, 2 labs); built a two-compartment spiking model of dendritic coincidence detection; embedded the multiplicative gate in an RL agent.
AI.result: Burst fraction encodes goal information far more selectively than firing rate (all p<10^-12). The mechanism is explained by a bilinear gate G(g)Y(s) from dendritic coincidence detection, enabling zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: Burst fraction is a goal-selective code in motor cortex, tied to a cellular mechanism that confers a computational learning advantage.

title: A kinetic model of shear-induced rupture of short dsDNA
comment: Supporting Information is provided at the end of the main text
summary: Force-induced dissociation of short double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) is central to single-molecule biophysics and DNA nanotechnology, yet a physically grounded kinetic description of shear-induced rupture for finite-length constructs remains lacking. Here we develop a master equation framework built on a force-dependent nucleation-zipper pathway with single-base transitions, enabling direct calculation of dissociation rates and transition state distances over a broad force range. Applied to a DNA-gold nanoparticle-DNA construct under constant shear force, the model accurately reproduces the experimental room-temperature data in the covered force regime and provides a unified interpretation of prior measurements on similarly sheared duplexes across all force regimes. A central result is that the three-dimensional helical geometry of dsDNA is essential for correctly defining the end to end distance under shear in the rod-like polymer model of short dsDNA. We further show that the extracted transition state distances are robust to variations in ssDNA polymer parameters within the experimentally relevant regime. Finally, we analyze the temperature dependence of the transition state distance and discuss how our framework captures globally-heated rupture while identifying the additional complications introduced by localized plasmonic heating in gold nanoparticle-coupled constructs. These results provide a predictive kinetic foundation for interpreting force-rupture experiments and for designing force- and temperature-actuated DNA nanostructures.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a master equation framework for force-induced dissociation of short dsDNA under shear, showing that helical geometry is essential for defining end-to-end distance, and accurately reproduces experimental data across force regimes.
AI.motivation: Force-induced dissociation of short dsDNA is central to single-molecule biophysics and DNA nanotechnology, yet a physically grounded kinetic description of shear-induced rupture for finite-length constructs remains lacking.
AI.method: The authors develop a master equation framework built on a force-dependent nucleation-zipper pathway with single-base transitions, enabling direct calculation of dissociation rates and transition state distances over a broad force range. Applied to a DNA-gold nanoparticle-DNA construct under constant shear force.
AI.result: The model accurately reproduces experimental room-temperature data in the covered force regime and provides a unified interpretation of prior measurements. A key finding is that the 3D helical geometry of dsDNA is essential for correctly defining end-to-end distance under shear in the rod-like polymer model.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a predictive kinetic foundation for interpreting force-rupture experiments and for designing force- and temperature-actuated DNA nanostructures. Transition state distances are robust to variations in ssDNA polymer parameters.

title: TRAPS: Therapeutic Response Analysis via Pathway-informed Stratification
summary: Cancer treatment planning requires decisions across multiple clinical dimensions at once. Clinicians must determine whether a patient should receive targeted molecular therapy, radiation therapy, and whether they are likely to survive beyond six months. Existing pathway-informed deep learning models have been developed and tested in isolation, making fair comparison across architectures impossible. We present the first unified benchmark for pathway-guided therapy response modeling, evaluating three biologically informed architectures, BINN, GraphPath, and PATH, across five cancer cohorts drawn from The Cancer Genome Atlas, representing 2,622 patients encoded using Reactome pathway activity scores. Each model is trained jointly on all three clinical outcomes under identical data and evaluation conditions, the first study to treat pathway-structured deep learning as a combined therapy and survival prediction problem. Our results show that no single architecture wins across all tasks: PATH performs best for targeted molecular therapy prediction overall, BINN is most reliable for survival prediction, and no model produces useful predictions for radiation therapy, as the key drivers of that decision are clinical variables not captured in gene expression data. Most strikingly, GraphPath achieves an AUROC of 0.92 on prostate targeted molecular therapy prediction, the highest score in the entire benchmark, demonstrating that lateral co-regulation structure produces exceptional discriminative power when matched to a cohort with a narrow targetable driver programme, even under conditions of extreme class imbalance at only 11\% positive prevalence.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first unified benchmark comparing three pathway-informed deep learning models (BINN, GraphPath, PATH) for combined therapy response and survival prediction across five cancer cohorts. No single model dominates all tasks: PATH excels at targeted therapy prediction, BINN at survival prediction, and GraphPath achieves exceptional performance (AUROC 0.92) on prostate targeted therapy prediction despite high class imbalance.
AI.motivation: Existing pathway-informed deep learning models have been developed and tested in isolation, making fair comparison across architectures impossible. The authors aim to provide a standardized benchmark for evaluating these models under identical conditions.
AI.method: Three biologically informed architectures (BINN, GraphPath, PATH) were trained jointly on three clinical outcomes (targeted molecular therapy, radiation therapy, six-month survival) across five TCGA cancer cohorts (2,622 patients) encoded using Reactome pathway activity scores, under identical data splits and evaluation conditions.
AI.result: No single architecture wins across all tasks: PATH best for targeted therapy prediction overall, BINN most reliable for survival prediction, and no model useful for radiation therapy prediction. GraphPath achieves exceptional AUROC of 0.92 on prostate targeted therapy prediction (11% positive prevalence).
AI.conclusion: Model selection should be task-specific rather than one-size-fits-all; gene expression data is insufficient for radiation therapy prediction; and GraphPath's lateral co-regulation structure provides strong discriminative power for narrow targetable driver programmes even under extreme class imbalance.

title: An information-geometric framework for mapping maximum potential biodiversity
summary: Biodiversity measures are often used descriptively: one computes a diversity index from an observed or estimated community composition and maps the resulting values across space. Conservation planning, however, also requires a site-specific benchmark against which the observed community can be compared. This chapter develops an information-geometric framework for such \emph{potential diversity} and the associated \emph{diversity gap}. The central object is a pair of probability vectors on the species simplex: an observed or realized composition \(p^{\mathrm{obs}}\), and a potential composition \(p^{\mathrm{pot}}\) obtained by a constrained variational principle. The gap is then defined by comparing a diversity functional at these two compositions. The framework is developed for both Hill-type diversity, which measures abundance and evenness, and Rao's quadratic entropy, which incorporates trait, phylogenetic, or ecological dissimilarities among species. A spatial point-process interpretation clarifies how local ecological capacities can be defined before passing to the simplex. Escort constraints, capacity constraints, and divergence projections then provide a unified way to define nontrivial benchmarks beyond the uniform distribution. The resulting formulation separates two distinct questions: how diverse a community is, and how far it is from a locally admissible potential benchmark. It also connects the ecological idea of dark diversity with a continuous, abundance-weighted comparison on the probability simplex. We also outline a dynamic extension in which capacities, species migration, and climate-driven shifts vary over time. Empirical implementation with large-scale citizen-science biodiversity data and trait databases is left for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an information-geometric framework for potential diversity and diversity gap, using constrained variational principles on the species simplex to compare observed vs. potential community compositions, covering Hill diversity and Rao's quadratic entropy.
AI.motivation: Current biodiversity measures are often used descriptively without a benchmark; conservation planning requires a site-specific comparison standard to assess whether observed communities deviate from their locally admissible potential.
AI.method: The framework uses information geometry on the probability simplex, defining potential composition via constrained variational principles (escort constraints, capacity constraints, divergence projections). It covers Hill-type diversity and Rao's quadratic entropy, with a spatial point-process interpretation for local ecological capacities.
AI.result: The formulation separates two distinct questions: (1) how diverse a community is, and (2) how far it is from a locally admissible potential benchmark. It connects dark diversity concepts with continuous abundance-weighted comparisons on the simplex.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified way to define nontrivial benchmarks beyond uniform distribution. Dynamic extensions for time-varying capacities, migration, and climate shifts are outlined; empirical implementation with citizen-science data is left for future work.

title: VFUSE: Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders
summary: Generative models have shown remarkable progress in a variety of domains such as protein design, but such power enables the opaque generation of hazardous proteins. In this work, we introduce VFUSE (Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders), a mechanistic interpretability approach that trains SAEs on diffusion-transformer activations to audit protein models for hazard-aware features. We apply VFUSE to RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, popular open-weight models for protein folding and synthesis. We find that for certain blocks, linear probes detect hazardous designs significantly better when fit in the SAE latent space over the original model's representations: improving interpretability without sacrificing model performance. Furthermore, we identify monosemantic features from the SAE that fire only on hazardous designs at up to AUROC $0.84$ ($q < 10^{-13}$). To our knowledge this is the first SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and the first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, paving the way towards safe and interpretable protein design.

AI.tldr: VFUSE trains sparse autoencoders on diffusion-transformer activations to audit protein design models (RoseTTAFold3, RFDiffusion3) for hazard-aware features, finding that SAE latents improve hazard detection interpretability and identifying monosemantic features with AUROC 0.84 for hazardous designs.
AI.motivation: Generative models can now produce hazardous proteins, yet their black-box nature makes it difficult to detect and audit such dangerous designs, motivating the need for interpretability tools to identify virulent features without sacrificing model performance.
AI.method: The authors train Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) on the activations of diffusion-transformer blocks in RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, then apply linear probes in the SAE latent space to detect hazardous protein designs, and identify monosemantic features that fire selectively on hazardous designs.
AI.result: Linear probes in SAE latent space detect hazardous designs better than using original model representations; monosemantic features achieve AUROC up to 0.84 (p < 10^-13) for hazard detection.
AI.conclusion: This is the first SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, paving the way towards safe and interpretable protein design.

title: Maximum Matching Accuracy: An Instance Segmentation Evaluation Metric Utilizing Globally Optimal Matching
summary: Reliable evaluation of instance segmentation models requires metrics that accurately and consistently reflect segmentation quality. However, the metrics most widely used in biological imaging carry fundamental mathematical weaknesses: hard Intersection-over-Union (IoU) thresholds that produce discontinuous, low sensitivity scoring; per-object normalization that distorts scores under object size variation; and greedy or one-to-many matching procedures that yield non-optimal, order-dependent correspondences. Together, these properties produce unintuitive and unreliable model rankings under common failure modes such as split cells, merged cells, and cell boundary imprecision. We propose Maximum Matching Accuracy (MMA), a threshold-free continuous metric that finds a globally optimal one-to-one matching between predicted and ground truth objects and aggregates total overlap using per-pixel normalization. We evaluate MMA against AP@50, PQ, SEG, and AJI across three experiments: synthetic failure cases, progressive corruption tests, and a model ranking comparison. MMA produces scores that are more stable, more sensitive, and more interpretable than existing alternatives, providing a principled foundation for fair instance segmentation benchmarking in biological cell imaging.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Maximum Matching Accuracy (MMA), a new metric for instance segmentation in biological imaging that overcomes fundamental weaknesses in existing metrics (AP@50, PQ, SEG, AJI) such as hard IoU thresholds, per-object normalization distortions, and non-optimal matching. MMA uses global optimal one-to-one matching with per-pixel normalization, producing more stable, sensitive, and interpretable scores.
AI.motivation: Existing instance segmentation metrics (AP@50, PQ, SEG, AJI) have fundamental mathematical weaknesses including discontinuous hard IoU thresholds, score distortion from per-object normalization under size variation, and non-optimal greedy/one-to-many matching, leading to unreliable model rankings under common failure modes like split/merged cells and boundary imprecision.
AI.method: MMA (Maximum Matching Accuracy) is a threshold-free continuous metric that uses globally optimal one-to-one matching between predictions and ground truth, with per-pixel normalization to aggregate total overlap.
AI.result: Evaluated across synthetic failure cases, progressive corruption tests, and model ranking comparisons, MMA produces more stable, sensitive, and interpretable scores than AP@50, PQ, SEG, and AJI.
AI.conclusion: MMA provides a principled foundation for fair instance segmentation benchmarking in biological cell imaging, addressing key reliability issues in existing metrics.

title: Mean-field models for morphogenetic processes in physiological contexts
summary: This work introduces a biophysical formalism to describe the spatiotemporal evolution of the chemical profile in tissues, with the novelty of modeling tissue compartmentalization and the mechanism by which cells maintain the system far from thermodynamic equilibrium via production and/or degradation of substances. The models were derived from conservation laws, chemical kinetic theory, and geometric constraints, while considering fundamental properties of tissues to connect theoretical modeling with experimental observations. In a morphogenetic context, each morphogen is described by two coupled reaction-diffusion equations, representing intra- and extracellular dynamics, linked through membrane transport processes such as nonlinear, cross, and anomalous diffusion. We explore the models' morphogenetic potential through diffusion-driven instabilities and discuss how natural tissue heterogeneities influence Turing instabilities and self-organized phenomena. The mathematical structure reveals that two-morphogen systems can produce Turing patterns with multiple characteristic length scales, while the system's dimensionality enables chaotic behavior in well-mixed dynamics. Moreover, due to domain coupling, Turing instabilities are allowed for single-morphogen systems. We used Schnakenberg kinetics to demonstrate that Turing patterns arise even when the activator diffuses faster than the inhibitor (d$<$1), thereby expanding the parameter space for pattern formation. Our results suggest that tissue spatial structure has important consequences for Turing instability mechanisms, in some cases weakening the usual conditions for its emergence while widening the possible patterns it can produce. The proposed framework offers a minimal mathematical basis to explore emergent dynamics in biological and synthetic contexts, with potential applications in developmental biology and tissue engineering.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a biophysical model for chemical profile evolution in tissues, incorporating compartmentalization and cellular non-equilibrium dynamics via coupled reaction-diffusion equations. It shows that tissue heterogeneity expands Turing pattern possibilities, enabling patterns with single morphogens, chaotic dynamics, and pattern formation even when activators diffuse faster than inhibitors.
AI.motivation: To develop a biophysical formalism that captures tissue compartmentalization and cellular active maintenance of non-equilibrium states, bridging theoretical modeling with experimental observations in morphogenesis and tissue engineering.
AI.method: Derived coupled reaction-diffusion equations for intra- and extracellular dynamics from conservation laws, chemical kinetic theory, and geometric constraints, incorporating membrane transport processes including nonlinear, cross, and anomalous diffusion.
AI.result: (1) Two-morphogen systems produce Turing patterns with multiple characteristic length scales; (2) System dimensionality enables chaotic behavior in well-mixed dynamics; (3) Domain coupling allows Turing instabilities for single-morphogen systems; (4) Turing patterns arise even when activator diffuses faster than inhibitor (d<1).
AI.conclusion: Tissue spatial structure has important consequences for Turing instability mechanisms, weakening usual conditions while widening possible patterns, offering a minimal mathematical basis for exploring emergent dynamics in developmental biology and tissue engineering.

title: Time-frequency localization of bird calls in dense soundscapes
summary: Passive acoustic monitoring enables large-scale observation of wildlife, but most bioacoustic classifiers only predict species presence in a time window without localizing vocalizations precisely in time or frequency, limiting downstream analyses. We formulate bird vocalization detection as an object detection task on spectrograms and train YOLO11 models to localize bird calls in dense tropical soundscapes from Singapore. We additionally introduce an open-source browser-based annotation tool and propose Intersection over Minimum (IoMin), an evaluation metric that better handles ambiguous acoustic boundaries than standard IoU and is better suited to the problem at hand. The best YOLO model nearly doubles baseline performance on in-distribution soundscapes from Singapore (81.8% vs. 42.1% IoMin@50 F1-score) while still outperforming the baseline on unseen out-of-distribution recordings from Hawaii (58.6% vs. 48.6%). These results suggest that object detection frameworks are a promising approach to time-frequency localization of animal vocalizations in complex soundscapes.

AI.tldr: This paper applies YOLO11 object detection to spectrograms for bird call localization in dense tropical soundscapes, introduces an open-source annotation tool and a new evaluation metric (IoMin), and achieves strong performance on both in-distribution (Singapore) and out-of-distribution (Hawaii) data.
AI.motivation: Most bioacoustic classifiers only predict species presence in a time window without precise time-frequency localization of vocalizations, limiting downstream analyses.
AI.method: Formulate bird vocalization detection as an object detection task on spectrograms using YOLO11 models; introduce an open-source browser-based annotation tool and a new evaluation metric called Intersection over Minimum (IoMin).
AI.result: Best YOLO model achieves 81.8% IoMin@50 F1-score on in-distribution Singapore data (vs. 42.1% baseline) and 58.6% on out-of-distribution Hawaii data (vs. 48.6% baseline).
AI.conclusion: Object detection frameworks are a promising approach for time-frequency localization of animal vocalizations in complex soundscapes.

title: A Comprehensive Inference-Time Augmentation Framework in Physiological Signals: Application to PPG-Based AF Detection
comment: 22 pages, 11 figures, 4 tables. Under review at Physiological Measurement
summary:
Objective: Accurate classification of physiological signals in real-world deployments is challenged by sensor noise, motion artifacts, and distribution shifts between training and deployment data. Inference-time augmentation (ITA), which applies augmentations during inference rather than retraining, offers a simple, model-agnostic mechanism to improve robustness. However, ITA application to physiological signals has remained narrow in scope, relying on limited augmentation methods with fixed, unoptimized parameters. This work proposes a unified ITA framework to address that gap.
  Approach: The framework incorporates 13 augmentation methods spanning time-domain, amplitude-domain, frequency-domain, and artifact-injection transformations, with hyperparameters optimized via Bayesian optimization. We evaluate on atrial fibrillation (AF) detection from 30-second PPG signals using GPT-PPG and ResNet across five datasets comprising more than 400 patients and ${\sim}$9,800 hours of recording.
  Main results: Standard ITA consistently improved AUROC (up to 8.5% for GPT-PPG and 0.7% for ResNet) and AUPRC (up to 10.6% for GPT-PPG and 0.8% for ResNet). Selective ITA further reduced average FPR by up to 4.4% (GPT-PPG) and 1.3% (ResNet) on non-AF datasets.
  Significance: These findings establish ITA as a practical, model-agnostic approach for improving PPG-based AF classification reliability in deployment settings where retraining is not feasible, with broader applicability to physiological signal analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified inference-time augmentation (ITA) framework with 13 augmentation methods and Bayesian optimization for hyperparameters, achieving up to 8.5% AUROC and 10.6% AUPRC improvements on PPG-based atrial fibrillation detection without retraining.
AI.motivation: Physiological signal classification in real-world settings suffers from sensor noise, motion artifacts, and distribution shifts. Existing ITA approaches use limited augmentation methods with fixed, unoptimized parameters.
AI.method: A unified ITA framework incorporating 13 augmentation methods across time-domain, amplitude-domain, frequency-domain, and artifact-injection transformations, with hyperparameters optimized via Bayesian optimization.
AI.result: Standard ITA improved AUROC (up to 8.5% for GPT-PPG, 0.7% for ResNet) and AUPRC (up to 10.6% for GPT-PPG, 0.8% for ResNet). Selective ITA reduced average FPR by up to 4.4% (GPT-PPG) and 1.3% (ResNet) on non-AF datasets.
AI.conclusion: ITA is a practical, model-agnostic approach for improving PPG-based AF classification reliability in deployment settings where retraining is infeasible, with broader applicability to physiological signal analysis.

title: Flexible Flows for Biological Sequence Design
summary: Designing functional biological sequences requires navigating vast discrete spaces under strict evolutionary and biophysical constraints. Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) offers a generative framework over such spaces, but existing approaches rely on biologically uninformative couplings and offer limited flexibility for variable-length sequence generation and fine-grained control. We propose a structured coupling that encodes domain-specific preferences among sequence elements, biasing the source distribution toward plausible regions without modifying the flow objective or training procedure. Building on this, we introduce a latent edit-based rate parameterization that models variable-length generation via edit operations conditioned on a shared global latent, akin to a latent variable model, while remaining tractable. We further introduce a latent classifier-free guidance mechanism that steers generation coherently in continuous latent space, along with Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling for test-time control over edit operations. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse biological sequence tasks, including density estimation, unconditional and conditional DNA sequence generation, and peptide sequence generation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a structured coupling, latent edit-based rate parameterization, latent classifier-free guidance, and Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling to improve Discrete Flow Matching for biological sequence generation, achieving SOTA results across DNA and peptide tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing Discrete Flow Matching methods rely on biologically uninformative couplings and lack flexibility for variable-length sequence generation and fine-grained control, limiting their applicability to functional biological sequence design.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) a structured coupling encoding domain-specific preferences, (2) a latent edit-based rate parameterization for variable-length generation, (3) latent classifier-free guidance for coherent steering in continuous latent space, and (4) Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling for test-time control over edit operations.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse biological sequence tasks including density estimation, unconditional and conditional DNA generation, and peptide sequence generation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating biologically informed structure and latent control mechanisms into discrete flow matching significantly improves generative performance and flexibility for functional biological sequence design.

title: Spontaneous polarization for protrusion-driven cell crawling
summary: We propose a minimal one-dimensional continuum model for the spontaneous initiation of protrusion-driven cell crawling on a rigid substrate. The cell cytoskeleton is represented as a viscous actin meshwork that turns over in the bulk and polymerizes at two moving cell edges. Symmetry breaking arises from the feedback between cell motion, an external chemical regulator of actin nucleation, and actin polymerization at the cell fronts. When the cell moves, the regulator becomes polarized around the moving boundaries, thereby imposing different actin nucleation densities at the two edges. This generates unequal protrusive rates, which in turn reinforce motion and sustain the chemical polarization. Above a critical protrusive activity, the static symmetric state loses stability and the system undergoes a bifurcation toward a motile polarized state. Depending on how the external cue controls actin nucleation, the transition can be either supercritical or subcritical, leading in the latter case to coexistence between static and motile states. Using parameter values appropriate for keratocyte cells, the model predicts realistic crawling speeds and actin-density profiles, including asymmetric edge-localized density peaks. These results identify a generic mechanism by which external biochemical regulation of actin nucleation can trigger spontaneous motility along a one-dimensional track without requiring molecular motors, specific adhesion dynamics, deformable substrates, or pre-existing polarity.

AI.tldr: A minimal 1D continuum model shows that feedback between cell motion and an external chemical regulator of actin nucleation can spontaneously trigger cell crawling without requiring molecular motors, adhesion dynamics, or pre-existing polarity.
AI.motivation: To identify a minimal, generic mechanism for spontaneous symmetry breaking and initiation of cell crawling that does not rely on molecular motors, specific adhesions, or substrate deformability.
AI.method: A one-dimensional continuum model of a viscous actin meshwork with bulk turnover, edge polymerization, and coupling to an external chemical regulator of actin nucleation. Bifurcation analysis is performed to study stability transitions.
AI.result: Above a critical protrusive activity, the static symmetric state bifurcates into a motile polarized state. The transition can be supercritical or subcritical depending on how the external cue regulates nucleation. The model predicts realistic crawling speeds and asymmetric actin-density profiles for keratocyte parameters.
AI.conclusion: External biochemical regulation of actin nucleation alone can drive spontaneous cell motility along a 1D track, offering a generic mechanism independent of molecular motors, adhesion dynamics, deformable substrates, or pre-existing polarity.

title: Flexible Kernels for Protein Property Prediction
summary: Despite its importance to applications in protein design, predicting protein properties like binding affinity and thermostability from sparse experimental data remains a significant challenge. Accordingly, we introduce a class of sequence kernels that exploit evolutionary substitution matrices as well as local linearity and demonstrate that the resulting Gaussian processes provide data-efficient models of protein property landscapes, frequently outperforming alternatives that rely on foundation model embeddings. Furthermore--by learning what are in effect structure-aware substitution matrices--we show that our kernels can readily incorporate structural information from foundation models. We demonstrate that these structure-conditioned kernels are well suited to multi-task learning across multiple protein property landscapes and can decisively outperform local supervised learning methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces sequence kernels combining evolutionary substitution matrices with local linearity, enabling data-efficient Gaussian process models for protein property prediction that outperform foundation model embeddings, and extends to structure-conditioned kernels for multi-task learning.
AI.motivation: Predicting protein properties (e.g., binding affinity, thermostability) from sparse experimental data is challenging but crucial for protein design applications.
AI.method: A class of sequence kernels leveraging evolutionary substitution matrices and local linearity within Gaussian process models, further extended to incorporate structural information from foundation models via learned structure-aware substitution matrices.
AI.result: The proposed kernels provide data-efficient models that frequently outperform alternatives using foundation model embeddings, and structure-conditioned kernels excel in multi-task learning across multiple protein property landscapes, outperforming local supervised learning.
AI.conclusion: The kernels offer a powerful, data-efficient approach for modeling protein properties, with structure-aware extensions enabling effective multi-task learning.

title: GRAFT: Gain-Recalibrated Adapters for Transformer-Based Neural Population Activity Modeling
summary: Neural population activity models can recover rich temporal structure from binned spikes, but their read-in and readout layers often remain tied to a fixed set of recorded neurons. This coupling limits reuse in long-term brain-computer interfaces, where recorded neuron identities, counts, and response statistics can change across days. We introduce GRAFT, a Transformer-based neural population activity model that separates reusable temporal dynamics from a recalibratable neuron interface. The neuron interface controls how recorded neurons enter and leave the shared backbone, and auxiliary gain and positional mechanisms support neural activity modeling inside the Transformer. On MC Maze under the standard NLB'21 protocol, GRAFT reaches 0.3866 co-bps as an ensemble, setting a new state of the art on the primary co-bps metric among public and reported NLB'21 results. In a cross-day protocol constructed from the NLB'21 MC Maze dataset series, GRAFT recalibrates from MC Maze to the scaled MC Maze datasets (Large/Medium/Small) by updating only 9.21% of parameters, reaching 0.3749, 0.3112, and 0.3152 co-bps with restricted target-day support sets. These results show that the same interface-backbone separation supports both strong Transformer-based neural population activity modeling and data-efficient cross-day recalibration.

AI.tldr: GRAFT is a Transformer-based neural population activity model that separates reusable temporal dynamics from a recalibratable neuron interface, achieving state-of-the-art performance on MC Maze and efficient cross-day recalibration by updating only 9.21% of parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing neural population activity models tie read-in/readout layers to a fixed set of recorded neurons, limiting reuse in long-term brain-computer interfaces where neuron identities, counts, and response statistics change across days.
AI.method: GRAFT uses a Transformer-based architecture with a neuron interface that controls how recorded neurons enter/leave a shared backbone, plus auxiliary gain and positional mechanisms for neural activity modeling inside the Transformer.
AI.result: GRAFT achieves 0.3866 co-bps on MC Maze (state-of-the-art under NLB'21 protocol) and reaches 0.3749, 0.3112, 0.3152 co-bps on cross-day recalibration tasks by updating only 9.21% of parameters.
AI.conclusion: The interface-backbone separation in GRAFT supports both strong Transformer-based neural population activity modeling and data-efficient cross-day recalibration.

title: QUIET: Quantifying Underutilized Influential Edges for Targeted Synchronization
summary: Network control theory can be used to model intrinsic and extrinsic strategies to steer neural dynamics. Standard approaches are node-centric, structural, and focused on achieving desired instantaneous states. Here, we develop an edge-centric approach which incorporates both structure and function to achieve extended patterns of neural dynamics characterized by desired synchronization states. Our method, Quantifying Underutilized Influential Edges for Targeted Synchronization (QUIET), is an edge-centric framework that integrates structural controllability of individual white matter connections and mutual information between pairwise functional timeseries to identify energy-efficient synchronization pathways. QUIET identifies quiet highways, edges that are structurally influential but functionally underutilized, to optimize regional synchronization. We validated QUIET across 75 synthetic configurations, where QUIET-ranked edge sets significantly outperformed random selection in 93% of cases (p<0.01). The framework, tested on Human Connectome Project participants, revealed that the control energy required for synchronization of the salience network correlates with fluid intelligence. QUIET, applied to healthy adults undergoing dexmedetomidine-induced unresponsiveness, showed that the frontoparietal and default-mode networks exhibited the largest control energy required for synchronization in both awake and sedated states. QUIET is released as a stand-alone software to be used to study theoretically-defined synchronization pathways, which in turn could inform testable hypotheses in perturbative studies.

AI.tldr: QUIET is an edge-centric network control framework that identifies structurally influential but functionally underutilized edges ("quiet highways") to efficiently synchronize neural dynamics, validated on synthetic data, HCP participants, and sedation datasets.
AI.motivation: Standard network control approaches are node-centric and focus on achieving instantaneous states, lacking methods to incorporate both structure and function for extended synchronization patterns.
AI.method: QUIET integrates structural controllability of white matter connections with mutual information between pairwise functional timeseries to identify energy-efficient synchronization pathways (quiet highways).
AI.result: QUIET-ranked edges significantly outperformed random selection in 93% of 75 synthetic configurations (p<0.01). Salience network synchronization energy correlates with fluid intelligence. Frontoparietal and default-mode networks showed largest control energy requirements in both awake and sedated states.
AI.conclusion: QUIET provides a theoretically-grounded, edge-centric framework to identify synchronization pathways that can inform testable hypotheses in perturbative brain studies.

title: OncoTraj: a public benchmark for longitudinal resistance prediction in EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer on osimertinib
comment: 24 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables. Code, data, and trained model weights: https://github.com/span-ai-labs/oncotraj. Python package: pip install oncotraj. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/span-ai-labs/oncotraj-v1
summary: Resistance to first-line osimertinib in EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the canonical example of predictable clonal evolution under therapeutic pressure, yet no public benchmark exists for training or evaluating computational models on the corresponding longitudinal patient trajectories. We introduce OncoTraj, a public benchmark of 813 EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients receiving first-line osimertinib, harmonized from three real-world clinical-genomic sources: MSK-CHORD (672 patients), AACR Project GENIE BPC NSCLC (34 patients), and the FLAURA molecular-resistance supplement (107 patients). OncoTraj defines three locked tasks: (A) binary classification of progression by a fixed 12-month landmark, (B) regression of time-to-first-progression in days, and (C) six-class classification of the dominant resistance mechanism. We release the harmonized dataset, patient-level train/validation/test splits with an audited no-leakage guarantee, an open-source evaluation harness, and six reference baselines spanning a majority-class predictor, logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, an LSTM, and a multi-task transformer. With v1's single-timepoint snapshot features, no task clears chance on clean within-source evaluation: the uniformity of this ceiling across every model class localizes the limit to the input modality (single-snapshot tissue NGS rather than serial ctDNA), not the algorithm. The benchmark does recover a reproducible literature-consistent association: TP53 co-mutation raises the 12-month progression rate from 29% to 59% cohort-wide. OncoTraj establishes a reproducible, leakage-audited baseline and converts the modality limit into concrete design requirements for a serial-ctDNA-enriched v2.

AI.tldr: OncoTraj is a public benchmark of 813 EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients on first-line osimertinib, defining three tasks (progression classification, time-to-progression regression, resistance mechanism classification). No model beats chance using single-timepoint features, revealing the input modality (single tissue NGS) as the bottleneck, not the algorithm. TP53 co-mutation association is replicated (29%→59% progression rate).
AI.motivation: Resistance to first-line osimertinib in EGFR-mutant NSCLC is a canonical example of predictable clonal evolution under therapeutic pressure, yet no public benchmark exists for training or evaluating computational models on longitudinal patient trajectories.
AI.method: Harmonized 813 patients from three real-world sources (MSK-CHORD, GENIE BPC, FLAURA), defined three locked tasks (12-month progression binary classification, time-to-progression regression, 6-class resistance mechanism classification), released with audited no-leakage splits and six baselines (majority-class, logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, LSTM, multi-task transformer).
AI.result: No task clears chance on clean within-source evaluation; the ceiling is uniform across all model classes, localizing the limit to the input modality (single-snapshot tissue NGS) rather than the algorithm. TP53 co-mutation raises 12-month progression rate from 29% to 59%.
AI.conclusion: OncoTraj establishes a reproducible, leakage-audited baseline and converts the modality limit into concrete design requirements for a serial-ctDNA-enriched v2 benchmark.
